subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — Hernandez v. Texas (1954)
stimulus:
In the 1950s, Pete Hernandez, a Mexican American agricultural worker, was found guilty of murder and sentenced to life in prison by an all-white jury in Jackson County, Texas. Hernandez's defense claimed that people of Mexican ancestry had been discriminated against in Jackson County. They pointed to the fact that no person of Mexican ancestry had served on a jury in 25 years and that the Jackson County Courthouse itself practiced segregation in its facilities. The five jury commissioners, who selected the members of the grand jury, testified under oath that they selected jurors based only on their qualifications and did not consider race or national origin in their decisions.

In the ensuing case, Hernandez v. Texas (1954), the Supreme Court unanimously ruled in favor of Hernandez, deciding that evidence of discrimination against Mexican Americans existed in Jackson County and that the Constitution prohibits such discrimination.

parts.prompt: Identify the clause in the Fourteenth Amendment that was used as the basis for the decision in both Brown v. Board of Education (1954) and Hernandez v. Texas (1954).
parts.scoring_notes: Only the Equal Protection Clause earns the point. The Due Process Clause does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Identify the clause in the Fourteenth Amendment that was used as the basis for the decision in both Brown v. Board of Education (1954) and Hernandez v. Texas (1954).

required_elements: Identifies the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment as the common constitutional basis
alternatives.correct_example: The Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment was the basis for both Brown v. Board of Education and Hernandez v. Texas.

wrong_examples: The Due Process Clause — while also in the Fourteenth Amendment, the equal protection guarantee is the specific clause used in both cases.
The First Amendment — neither case involved First Amendment rights.
common_traps: Accept 'Equal Protection Clause' or 'equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment' — do not require the full amendment number
Do not accept 'due process' — the equal protection clause is the correct answer

parts.prompt: Explain how the facts in both Brown v. Board of Education and Hernandez v. Texas led to a similar decision in both cases.
parts.scoring_notes: First point requires a specific fact or holding from Brown (not just the name of the case). Second point requires connecting facts from BOTH cases to explain the similar outcome — systematic racial/ethnic discrimination in both violated the Equal Protection Clause.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in both cases led to a similar decision. (1 point — describe relevant fact or holding about the required Supreme Court case Brown v. Board of Education)

required_elements: Accurately describes a fact or holding from Brown v. Board of Education, the required case
alternatives.correct_example: In Brown v. Board of Education, the Supreme Court held that racially segregated public schools were inherently unequal and violated the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.

required_elements: Accurately describes a fact or holding from Brown v. Board of Education, the required case
alternatives.correct_example: Brown involved African American students who were denied admission to white public schools based on their race, which the Court found unconstitutionally discriminatory.

wrong_examples: Describes only the Hernandez facts without mentioning Brown — earns 0 points.
Confuses Brown with Plessy v. Ferguson, which upheld rather than struck down segregation.
common_traps: Only Brown information is needed for the first point
Both the facts and the holding earn the point

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in both cases led to a similar decision. (2 points — correctly explains how facts of BOTH cases led to similar holdings)

required_elements: Explains how facts from both cases involved systematic racial or ethnic discrimination, and how both courts applied the Equal Protection Clause to strike down that discrimination
alternatives.correct_example: In Brown, segregated schools denied African American children equal educational opportunities based solely on race, violating the Equal Protection Clause. In Hernandez, Mexican Americans were systematically excluded from jury service in Jackson County for 25 years, also a form of ethnic discrimination that the Court found violated the Equal Protection Clause. Both cases reached similar holdings because both involved systematic exclusion of a racial or ethnic group from full participation in public institutions.

wrong_examples: Describes only one case in detail — earns 1 point (b1) only.
States both cases were about 'discrimination' without explaining how the specific facts in each led to the same legal outcome.
common_traps: Must address BOTH cases to earn the second point
The key similarity is that both cases involved systematic discrimination against a racial or ethnic group that violated the Equal Protection Clause

parts.prompt: Explain how an interest group could use the decision in Hernandez v. Texas to advance its agenda.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any plausible strategy an interest group could use to leverage the Hernandez precedent. The response must explain how the decision would be used, not just state that the group could use it.

scoring_points.description: Explain how an interest group could use the decision in Hernandez v. Texas to advance its agenda.

required_elements: Identifies a specific strategy and explains how the Hernandez decision would enable or support that strategy
alternatives.correct_example: A civil rights organization representing Latino communities could file amicus curiae briefs in future cases citing Hernandez v. Texas to argue that the Equal Protection Clause prohibits discrimination against Hispanic Americans in jury selection, employment, or other public settings.

required_elements: Identifies a specific strategy and explains how the Hernandez decision would enable or support that strategy
alternatives.correct_example: An interest group focused on equal representation could lobby Congress to pass legislation expanding civil rights protections for minority groups by using the Hernandez precedent to demonstrate that the Constitution requires equal treatment for all ethnic groups.

required_elements: Identifies a specific strategy and explains how the Hernandez decision would enable or support that strategy
alternatives.correct_example: A civil rights interest group could sponsor litigation in cases where minorities are systematically excluded from jury service, using the Hernandez precedent to argue that such exclusion violates the Equal Protection Clause.

wrong_examples: Simply stating an interest group 'would use the case' without explaining how or what they would do.
Describing a general lobbying strategy with no connection to the Hernandez ruling.
common_traps: Accept any of the classic interest group strategies (litigation, lobbying, amicus briefs, grassroots organizing, media campaigns) as long as the response connects the strategy to the Hernandez holding
The response must explain how the decision is used — not just that a group would reference it
